description: This figure presents the iron-rich corner of the Fe-Mn-Al ternary phase diagram for alloys with ~1 wt% C, characterized at 1150 °C. The horizontal axis shows Mn content ranging 0 to 50 wt%, the left vertical axis shows Al content ranging 0 to 30 wt%, with Fe as the balance element. Two composition regions are defined: region 1 contains alloys with single-phase γ-solid solution (austenite) structure, region 2 contains alloys with two-phase γ + α-solid solution (austenite + ferrite) structure, and line a-b is the phase boundary between the two regions. This diagram supports composition optimization of Fe-Mn-Al alloys, to achieve desired single-phase or duplex microstructure for targeted performance.

This set of four optical micrographs presents the microstructure of two Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys under different heat treatment conditions: alloy I (25 wt% Mn, 10 wt% Al, 1 wt% C) and alloy II (20 wt% Mn, 10 wt% Al, 1 wt% C). Subfigure a shows samples after quenching from 1150 °C, imaged at ×150 magnification, exhibiting uniform polygonal grain structures with negligible intergranular precipitates. Subfigure b shows samples after quenching followed by aging at 600 °C for 50 h, imaged at ×500 magnification, where obvious grain boundary precipitates (carbides and dispersed phases), retained austenite, and ferrite phases are observed. Comparison reveals that aging triggers γ phase decomposition and grain boundary precipitation, which corresponds to the hardness increase from HRB 86-88 to HRC 35-37, and higher Mn content correlates with higher retained austenite fraction after aging. This figure verifies that Mn content and heat treatment dominate phase composition and precipitate distribution in these alloys, directly tuning their mechanical properties.

This figure displays the mechanical property evolution of an Fe-Mn-Al austenitic alloy with nominal composition 27.5 wt% Mn, 9.1 wt% Al, and 0.92 wt% C as a function of 16 h isothermal aging temperature. The x-axis is aging temperature in °C, the y-axis reports two types of parameters: ultimate tensile strength (σ_b) and 0.2% offset yield strength (σ_0.2) in kg/mm², plus elongation (δ) and reduction in area (ψ) in %. Both σ_b and σ_0.2 increase continuously with aging temperature up to 550 °C, peaking at ~95 kg/mm² and ~85 kg/mm² respectively, then decrease slightly above 550 °C. Conversely, δ and ψ decrease with increasing aging temperature to 550 °C, reaching minima of ~10% and ~10% respectively, before partially recovering at temperatures above 550 °C. The figure supports the conclusion that peak strength occurs at 550 °C from dispersed phase precipitation, while aging above 550 °C reduces strength and restores plasticity via precipitate coalescence.
